english: 1. v. na (na) I. 2. also ná-mo (na mo) (नमस्), praise, glory, adoration, na-mo gu-ru (na mo gu ru) praise to the teacher!
Ssk. = z̀es byá-ba (zhes bya ba) so called, frq. in titles of books.
(*ná-za* (*na za*), vulg. *náb-za* (*nab za*), *núm-za* (*num za*)) resp. for gos (gos), garment, dress, frq.; gsól-ba (gsol ba) to put it on.
obs. or vulg. for na-bún (na bun), old edition of Mil.
medicinal herb, Med.; in Lh. Polygon. viviparum.

by form and position an adv., like _di-skad-du ('di skad du); before words or sentences that are quoted literally, mostly followed by smrás-nas (smras nas), zér-ba-la (zer ba la), but not always, in which latter case it stands for 'he says, he said' etc., the noun being always put in the nom. case, never in the instr.: _pags-pa na-re ('phags pa na re) the Reverend said; rarely in accessory sentences: gál-te gzán-dag ná-re (gal te gzan dag na re) (not ná-re-na (na re na)) si forte alii dixerint Wdn.; even without gál-te (gal te) in the same sense Thgy. It hardly occurs in old classical literature, nor in the col. language of W., but pretty frq. in later literature. In Kun., however, there exists a vb. ná-čas (na chas) (*ná-čà* (*na cha*)), pf. nas (nas) (*nā* (*na*)), imp. nos (nos) (*no* (*no*)) which is used for zér-ba (zer ba) (not in use there), and is construed with the instr.: ă-pa-su nā soṅ (a pa su na song) the father has said.
the sign for the vowel o, ~.
n. of a holy Lama Mil.; nā-ro-pa (na ro pa) Tar. 181, 10 id.? ná-roi sems-_dzin-gyi lčags-ťág (na ro'i sems 'dzin gyi lcags thag) a sort of puzzle.
Pth. nā-len-dra (na len dra) Wdk., n. of a monastery in Magadha.
bowl, basin, an iron or china dish W.
Lt., šal (shal) S.g., = ši-kru (shi kru) Wdn. (शिग्रु?) n. of an acrid medicine.
(blackness?) crime, offence, transgression, v. nág-pa (nag pa) comp.; nag-ku-be-ra (nag ku be ra) v. ku-be-ra (ku be ra).

black, ber pyi nág-pa naṅ dkár-ba (ber phyi nag pa nang dkar ba) a garment outside black, inside white Glr.; *nág-po ma ku* (*nag po ma ku*) do not blacken it, do not soil it! of the countenance dark, frowning, gloomy, mournful Glr.; mi nag (mi nag) (-po (-po) or -pa (-pa)) a black one, a layman, (on account of his not being clad in a red or yellow clerical garb); nág-po (nag po) n. p. Krishna Tar., nág-po čén-po (nag po chen po) = महाकाल Siwa; nág-mo (nag mo) 1. a black woman, 2. Kali, Uma; nág-moi-_baṅs (nag mo'i 'bangs) or kol (khol) Kālidāsa. — 3. woman, in general Sch. — nag-_grós (nag 'gros), nág-po _gro-sès (nag po 'gro shes) 'easy to be understood' Sch.; acc. to our Lama from Tashilunpo nág-po _gro-bsèr (nag po 'gro bser) implies: illustrating a sentence by comparing it with similar passages; nág-čan (nag can) 1. a person guilty of a crime Sch.; mi nág-čan dóṅ-nas târ-pa (mi nag can dong nas thar pa) a criminal released from prison Mil. 2. a married man Sch. — nag-pyúgs (nag phyugs) black-cattle, horned cattle Sch.; v. also gnág-pa (gnag pa). — nág-ču (nag chu) n. of a river north of Lhasa, Huc II, 238; nág-ču-kă-pa (nag chu kha pa) people living on its banks, notorious for their thievish propensities. — nag-čén (nag chen), nag-nyès (nag nyes) C. a heinous crime. — nag-túm (nag thum), nag-tóm (nag thom), Sch., nag-siṅ-ba (nag sing ba) Thgy., nag-hur-ré (nag hur re) Sch., coal-black, jet-black. — nag-nóg (nag nog) (-čan (-can)) dirty, dingy; not clear, as bad print; fig. stained, polluted, with sin, guilt, sems (sems). — nag-pyógs (nag phyogs) v. pyógs (phyogs). — nag(-ma)-tsúr (nag ma tshur) a black mineral colour, Sch : green vitriol(?). — nag-tsíg (nag tshig) a point, dot, W. — nag-zúg (nag zug) (?) darkness, nag-zúg-la snóm-bzin soṅ (nag zug la snom bzhin song) he groped about in the dark.
Sch.: linden-tree, lime-tree (hardly to be found in Tibet; the word perhaps introduced from Mongol dictionaries).

B., C., W., forest, rtsi-šiṅ-nags-kyis mdzes (rtsi shing nags kyis mdzes) beautified by forests, richly wooded Glr.; _túg-po ('thug po) dense forest; nags-kród (nags khrod) a thicket Glr.; nágs-čan (nags can) woody, covered with forests; nags-ljóṅs (nags ljongs) woodland country, a well-wooded province; nags-sbál (nags sbal) Lt. tree-frog (?); nags-tsál (nags tshal) = nags (nags), nyám-ṅa-ba (nyam nga ba) a dreadful forest Dzl.; yíd-du-_òṅ-ba (yid du 'ong ba) a lovely wood Sambh.; nags-(g)séb (nags gseb) an intersected forest, v. (g)séb (gseb).
